content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 4, 2007

Karen L. Moreno, Filing Officer
California Department of Health Services
Personnel Management Branch
MS 1300-1302
P.O. Box 997411
Sacramento, CA 95899-7411

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-220

Dear Ms. Moreno:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the Department of Health
Services for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Because you have asked general questions relating to the procedures
for adopting and amending conflict-of-interest codes, we are treating your letter as a
request for informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS AND DISCUSSION

1. Senate Bill 162, signed by the Governor on September 14, 2006, enacts the
California Public Health Act of 2006. The bill splits the responsibilities of the
Department of Health Services to establish the State Department of Public Health and to
rename the Department of Health Services to the State Department of Health Care
Services effective July 1, 2007. You have asked about the procedures for amending the
conflict-of-interest code of the Department of Health Services and adopting a conflict-of-
interest code for the Department of Public Health.

Section 87300 requires every agency to adopt and promulgate a conflict-of-
interest code. A new agency must adopt a code no later than six months after it comes

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c).)

File No. 1-06-220
Page No. 2

into existence. (Section 87303.) An existing agency must amend its code within 90 days
when necessitated by changed circumstances, including renaming the agency, adding or
deleting designated positions, and revising disclosure categories. (Section 87306.)
Regulation 18750 (newly enclosed) establishes specific procedures for adopting or
amending a conflict-of-interest code. This regulation also provides the time lines for
performing certain tasks. Section 87303 also states that a code is not effective until it has
been approved by the code reviewing body. For state agencies, the Commission is the
code reviewing body. (Section 82011(a).) Thus, the documents needed to amend and
adopt a conflict-of-interest code must be submitted to the Commission for approval.

With regard to employees designated in the conflict-of-interest code of the
Department of Health Services who are moving to the newly-created Department of
Public Health, you may find the enclosed letters helpful in determining whether it will be
necessary to seek written advice from the Commission regarding their ongoing filing
requirements. (Duveneck Advice Letter, No. A-05-133; Matuoka Advice Letter, No. A-
05-148; Stevens and Yonehiro Advice Letter, No. A-06-176.)

2. You have asked whether amendments to the conflict-of-interest code for the
Department of Health Services and the new conflict-of-interest code for the Department
of Public Health should be submitted at the same time or separately. Nothing in the Act
requires that the two actions be taken or submitted simultaneously. As noted above,
depending on the timing of the amendments and adoption of the new code, you may wish
to seek specific advice regarding the ongoing filing requirements for designated
employees who will be moving to the new agency.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosures

S AND DISCUSSION

1. Senate Bill 162, signed by the Governor on September 14, 2006, enacts the California Public Health Act of 2006. The bill splits the responsibilities of the Department of Health Services to establish the State Department of Public Health and to rename the Department of Health Services to the State Department of Health Care Services effective July 1, 2007. You have asked about the procedures for amending the conflict-of-interest code of the Department of Health Services and adopting a conflict-of-interest code for the Department of Public Health.

Section 87300 requires every agency to adopt and promulgate a conflict-of-interest code. A new agency must adopt a code no later than six months after it comes

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c).)

into existence. (Section 87303.) An existing agency must amend its code within 90 days when necessitated by changed circumstances, including renaming the agency, adding or deleting designated positions, and revising disclosure categories. (Section 87306.) Regulation 18750 (copy enclosed) establishes specific procedures for adopting or amending a conflict-of-interest code. This regulation also provides the time lines for performing certain tasks. Section 87303 also states that a code is not effective until it has been approved by the code reviewing body. For state agencies, the Commission is the code reviewing body. (Section 82011(a).) Thus, the documents needed to amend and adopt a conflict-of-interest code must be submitted to the Commission for approval.

With regard to employees designated in the conflict-of-interest code of the Department of Health Services who are moving to the newly-created Department of Public Health, you may find the enclosed letters helpful in determining whether it will be necessary to seek written advice from the Commission regarding their ongoing filing requirements. (Duveneck Advice Letter, No. A-05-133; Matuoka Advice Letter, No. A-05-148; Stevens and Yonehiro Advice Letter, No. A-06-176.)

2. You have asked whether amendments to the conflict-of-interest code for the Department of Health Services and the new conflict-of-interest code for the Department of Public Health should be submitted at the same time or separately. Nothing in the Act requires that the two actions be taken or submitted simultaneously. As noted above, depending on the timing of the amendments and adoption of the new code, you may wish to seek specific advice regarding the ongoing filing requirements for designated employees who will be moving to the new agency.
